static.description: A polyglot microservices platform for food delivery, consisting of 5 independent services (API Gateway, Order, Inventory, Driver, Notification) built across Kotlin, Java, Python, Go, and TypeScript, orchestrated via Docker Compose with RabbitMQ as the async event bus.
domain_concepts: OrderStatus: Enum representing the lifecycle states of an order: PENDING → ACCEPTED → PREPARING → READY_FOR_PICKUP → IN_TRANSIT → DELIVERED, with CANCELLED and REFUNDED as terminal/side states.
OrderItem: An embeddable line-item within an Order, containing a menu item reference, name, quantity, and unit price.
MenuItem: A restaurant menu item with price and stock quantity; stock_quantity of -1 means unlimited availability, otherwise finite stock must be reserved via Redis distributed lock.
Restaurant: A food establishment entity in the inventory service with menu items, address, cuisine type, and availability status.
Driver: A delivery driver with profile information (name, phone, vehicle type), availability status, and GPS location stored in MongoDB.
GeoPoint: A struct storing a driver's current latitude and longitude coordinates in MongoDB.
JwtAuthFilter: A global Spring Cloud Gateway filter that validates JWT tokens, extracts the customer ID from the 'sub' claim, and forwards it as the X-Customer-Id header to downstream services.
order.events: The RabbitMQ exchange used for publishing order lifecycle events that the notification service consumes.
acquire_lock: A Redis-based distributed lock mechanism in the inventory service to prevent concurrent overselling of finite-stock menu items.
PaymentIntent: A Stripe PaymentIntent created with manual capture at order creation time, used for payment capture and refund operations throughout the order lifecycle.
isValidTransition: A method on OrderStatus that enforces valid state machine transitions in the order lifecycle, preventing illegal status changes.

dynamic.architecture_summary: The system follows a microservices architecture with an API Gateway (Kotlin/Spring Cloud Gateway) handling JWT authentication and routing requests to three backend services: Order Service (Java/Spring Boot with PostgreSQL and Stripe), Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI with PostgreSQL and Redis distributed locks), and Driver Service (Go with MongoDB). The Notification Service (Node.js/TypeScript) consumes order lifecycle events from RabbitMQ and delivers SMS/email notifications via Twilio and SendGrid.

key_components.name: GatewayApplication
key_components.responsibility: Entry point for the Spring Cloud Gateway API Gateway that routes requests and applies JWT authentication.

key_components.name: JwtAuthFilter
key_components.responsibility: Global gateway filter that validates JWT tokens and forwards the authenticated customer ID as X-Customer-Id header to downstream services.

key_components.name: OrderServiceApplication
key_components.responsibility: Entry point for the Order Service Spring Boot application.

key_components.name: OrderController
key_components.responsibility: REST controller handling order CRUD operations and status transitions.

key_components.name: OrderService
key_components.responsibility: Business logic for order creation (with Stripe payment), status transitions, cancellation/refund, and publishing order events to RabbitMQ.

key_components.name: Order
key_components.responsibility: JPA entity representing a customer order with line items, status, total amount, and Stripe PaymentIntent ID.

key_components.name: OrderStatus
key_components.responsibility: Enum defining order lifecycle states and valid state transitions.

key_components.name: inventory-service/main.py
key_components.responsibility: FastAPI application handling restaurant/menu queries and stock reservation with Redis distributed locks.

key_components.name: models.py
key_components.responsibility: SQLAlchemy ORM models for Restaurant and MenuItem entities with PostgreSQL UUID primary keys.

key_components.name: driver-service/cmd/driver/main.go
key_components.responsibility: Go HTTP server for driver profile management and location tracking, backed by MongoDB with graceful shutdown.

key_components.name: notification-service/src/index.ts
key_components.responsibility: TypeScript service that consumes RabbitMQ order events and sends SMS (Twilio) and email (SendGrid) notifications with idempotency tracking.

agent_safe_operations: Add new REST endpoints to existing service controllers
Add new unit or integration tests to any service
Modify business logic in OrderService, inventory main.py, or driver main.go
Add new model fields to Order.java, models.py, or Driver struct (with corresponding migrations)
Update application configuration in application.properties or application.yml
Add new Python dependencies to requirements.txt
Add new npm dependencies to notification-service/package.json
Add new Go dependencies via go.mod
Add new Maven dependencies to order-service/pom.xml
Refactor notification message templates in notification-service/src/index.ts
Add new RabbitMQ event consumers or producers within existing services
Create new API routes in the API Gateway application.yml routing config
agent_forbidden_operations: Modify .env or .env.example with real secrets or credentials
Modify docker-compose.yml infrastructure service definitions without explicit approval
Modify the openapi.yaml specification without coordinating across all services
Change the RabbitMQ exchange name 'order.events' without updating all consumers and producers
Modify the JWT secret or authentication mechanism without updating all services
Change database schemas without providing proper migration scripts
Modify CI/CD workflow files without explicit approval
Edit lock files (package-lock.json, go.sum) directly — these should be auto-generated
Remove or rename existing OrderStatus enum values as they are used across services
Change inter-service communication contracts (X-Customer-Id header, event payload shapes) without updating all dependent services
potential_pitfalls: Each service uses a different language and build system — changes must target the correct toolchain (Maven for order-service, pip for inventory, go mod for driver, npm for notification, Gradle for api-gateway)
The inventory service uses async SQLAlchemy with asyncpg — all database calls must be awaited and use AsyncSession, not synchronous SQLAlchemy patterns
The Order entity uses @ElementCollection for OrderItem which creates a separate table — adding/removing items requires careful JPA collection management
Stock reservation in inventory-service uses Redis distributed locks — any code touching stock_quantity must acquire/release locks properly or risk overselling
OrderStatus.isValidTransition enforces a strict state machine — attempting invalid transitions will throw IllegalStateException
The API Gateway strips the Authorization header and replaces it with X-Customer-Id — downstream services rely on this header, not JWT directly
The driver-service is a single-file Go application with no framework — routing is manual switch/case, not a router library
The notification-service uses an in-memory Set for idempotency which does not survive restarts — this is a known limitation noted for production Redis replacement
Stripe PaymentIntent is created with MANUAL capture — the payment must be explicitly captured or it will expire, which affects the order lifecycle logic
The api-gateway uses Gradle (build.gradle.kts) while order-service uses Maven (pom.xml) — do not confuse the two Java/Kotlin build systems
Environment variable names differ across services (e.g., DATABASE_URL for Python vs SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL for Java) — use the correct variable names per service
